concepts.name: s-Block Elements – Introduction and Electronic Configuration
concepts.explanation: The s-block elements are those in which the last electron enters the outermost s-orbital. Group 1 (alkali metals) have ns¹ configuration, and Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) have ns² configuration. They are highly reactive metals.

concepts.name: Alkali Metals – Electronic Configuration and Occurrence
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) have the general electronic configuration [noble gas] ns¹. Sodium and potassium are abundant; lithium, rubidium, and caesium are less abundant. Francium is radioactive with a half-life of 21 minutes.

concepts.name: Alkaline Earth Metals – Electronic Configuration and Occurrence
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metals (Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra) have the general electronic configuration [noble gas] ns². Calcium and magnesium rank fifth and sixth in abundance in the earth's crust. Radium is radioactive.

concepts.name: Diagonal Relationship in s-Block
concepts.explanation: Lithium shows similarities to magnesium, and beryllium shows similarities to aluminium (diagonal relationship). This is due to similar ionic sizes and charge/radius ratios. Li resembles Mg, and Be resembles Al.

concepts.name: Atomic and Ionic Radii of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkali metal atoms have the largest sizes in their respective periods. Atomic and ionic radii increase down the group from Li to Cs. The monovalent ions (M⁺) are smaller than the parent atoms.

concepts.name: Ionisation Enthalpy of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals have low ionization enthalpies due to the loosely held ns¹ electron. Ionization enthalpy decreases down the group from Li to Cs because the effect of increasing size outweighs the increasing nuclear charge.

concepts.name: Hydration Enthalpy of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Hydration enthalpies of alkali metal ions decrease with increasing ionic size: Li⁺ > Na⁺ > K⁺ > Rb⁺ > Cs⁺. Li⁺ has the maximum degree of hydration; lithium salts are mostly hydrated (e.g., LiCl·2H₂O).

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals are silvery white, soft, light metals with low density (increases down the group, but K is lighter than Na). Melting and boiling points are low. They and their salts impart characteristic flame colours: Li (crimson red), Na (yellow), K (violet), Rb (red violet), Cs (blue).

concepts.name: Flame Test of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals impart characteristic colours to flames due to excitation of the outermost electron to a higher energy level. When the electron returns to the ground state, radiation in the visible region is emitted. Used for detection by flame photometry.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Reactivity towards Air
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals tarnish in dry air forming oxides, which react with moisture to form hydroxides. Li forms Li₂O (monoxide); Na forms Na₂O₂ (peroxide); K, Rb, Cs form superoxides (MO₂). Li also forms Li₃N with nitrogen. They are kept in kerosene oil.
concepts.formula_reaction: 4Li + O₂ → 2Li₂O; 2Na + O₂ → Na₂O₂; M + O₂ → MO₂ (M = K, Rb, Cs)

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Reactivity towards Water
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals react with water to form hydroxides and hydrogen gas: 2M + 2H₂O → 2M⁺ + 2OH⁻ + H₂. Although Li has the most negative E° value, its reaction with water is less vigorous than Na due to its small size and high hydration energy.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Reactivity towards Dihydrogen and Halogens
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals react with H₂ at ~673 K (Li at 1073 K) to form ionic hydrides (M⁺H⁻). They react vigorously with halogens to form ionic halides (M⁺X⁻). Lithium halides are somewhat covalent due to high polarisation capability of Li⁺ (LiI is most covalent).

concepts.name: Reducing Nature of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals are strong reducing agents. Li is the most powerful (most negative E° value) due to its high hydration enthalpy. Na is the least powerful. Reducing power order: Li > Cs > Rb > K > Na.

concepts.name: Solutions of Alkali Metals in Liquid Ammonia
concepts.explanation: Alkali metals dissolve in liquid ammonia giving deep blue solutions (paramagnetic) due to ammoniated electrons. The blue colour is due to the electron absorbing visible light. On standing, hydrogen is liberated forming amides. Concentrated solutions are bronze-coloured and diamagnetic.

concepts.name: Uses of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Li: alloys for aircraft parts, thermonuclear reactions, electrochemical cells. Na: Na/Pb alloy for organolead compounds, coolant in fast breeder reactors. K: biological role, KCl fertiliser, KOH for soft soap, CO₂ absorbent. Cs: photoelectric cells.

concepts.name: Oxides and Hydroxides of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Li forms Li₂O (and some Li₂O₂), Na forms Na₂O₂, K/Rb/Cs form superoxides MO₂. Larger cations stabilise larger anions through lattice energy. Oxides and peroxides are colourless; superoxides are yellow/orange and paramagnetic. They hydrolyse to hydroxides.
concepts.formula_reaction: M₂O + H₂O → 2MOH; M₂O₂ + 2H₂O → 2MOH + H₂O₂; 2MO₂ + 2H₂O → 2MOH + H₂O₂ + O₂

concepts.name: Halides of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkali metal halides (MX) are high melting, colourless crystalline solids. ΔfH° for fluorides becomes less negative down the group; reverse for chlorides, bromides, iodides. Solubility: LiF is insoluble due to high lattice enthalpy; CsI is insoluble due to low hydration enthalpy. LiCl is soluble in pyridine.

concepts.name: Salts of Oxoacids of Alkali Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkali metal salts of oxoacids (carbonates, sulphates, nitrates) are generally soluble in water and thermally stable. Carbonates become more stable down the group. Li₂CO₃ is less stable (decomposes to Li₂O + CO₂) due to small size polarising the carbonate ion. LiHCO₃ does not exist as a solid.

concepts.name: Anomalous Properties of Lithium
concepts.explanation: Lithium shows anomalous behaviour due to its exceptionally small size, high polarising power, and increased covalent character. Li is harder, has higher m.p./b.p., is least reactive but strongest reducing agent, forms Li₂O and Li₃N on combustion, LiCl is deliquescent and hydrated.

concepts.name: Similarities between Lithium and Magnesium
concepts.explanation: Li and Mg show diagonal relationship due to similar sizes (atomic: Li 152 pm, Mg 160 pm; ionic: Li⁺ 76 pm, Mg²⁺ 72 pm). Similarities: both are harder and lighter, react slowly with water, form nitrides, carbonates decompose on heating, chlorides are soluble in ethanol and deliquescent.

concepts.name: Sodium Carbonate (Washing Soda) – Solvay Process
concepts.explanation: Na₂CO₃·10H₂O is prepared by the Solvay process. NaCl solution saturated with NH₃ is treated with CO₂ to precipitate NaHCO₃ (low solubility). NaHCO₃ is heated to give Na₂CO₃. NH₃ is recovered from NH₄Cl using Ca(OH)₂. CaCl₂ is a by-product.
concepts.formula_reaction: NH₃ + H₂O + CO₂ → NH₄HCO₃; NH₄HCO₃ + NaCl → NaHCO₃ + NH₄Cl; 2NaHCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + CO₂ + H₂O

concepts.name: Properties and Uses of Sodium Carbonate
concepts.explanation: Na₂CO₃·10H₂O is a white crystalline solid (washing soda). On heating, it loses water to form monohydrate, then anhydrous soda ash. The carbonate ion hydrolyses to give an alkaline solution. Uses: water softening, glass manufacture, soap, borax, caustic soda, paper, textile, and as a laboratory reagent.
concepts.formula_reaction: Na₂CO₃·10H₂O → Na₂CO₃·H₂O + 9H₂O (373K); CO₃²⁻ + H₂O → HCO₃⁻ + OH⁻

concepts.name: Sodium Chloride – Occurrence and Purification
concepts.explanation: NaCl is obtained from sea water (2.7-2.9% by mass) by evaporation. Crude salt contains impurities (Na₂SO₄, CaSO₄, CaCl₂, MgCl₂). It is purified by dissolving in water, filtering, and saturating with HCl gas. Pure NaCl crystallises out; impurities remain in solution.

concepts.name: Sodium Hydroxide (Caustic Soda) – Castner-Kellner Process
concepts.explanation: NaOH is prepared by electrolysis of brine (NaCl solution) using a mercury cathode and carbon anode. Na metal discharged at the cathode forms Na-amalgam. Chlorine is evolved at the anode. The amalgam is treated with water to give NaOH and H₂.
concepts.formula_reaction: Cathode: Na⁺ + e⁻ → Na(Hg); Anode: Cl⁻ → ½Cl₂ + e⁻; 2Na(Hg) + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + 2Hg + H₂

concepts.name: Properties and Uses of Sodium Hydroxide
concepts.explanation: NaOH is a white, translucent, deliquescent solid (m.p. 591 K). It is readily soluble in water, forming a strong alkaline solution. It reacts with CO₂ to form Na₂CO₃. Uses: soap, paper, artificial silk, petroleum refining, purification of bauxite, textile mercerising, and as a laboratory reagent.

concepts.name: Sodium Hydrogencarbonate (Baking Soda)
concepts.explanation: NaHCO₃ (baking soda) is prepared by saturating Na₂CO₃ solution with CO₂. NaHCO₃ precipitates due to low solubility. On heating, it decomposes to give CO₂ (used in baking). It is a mild antiseptic and is used in fire extinguishers.
concepts.formula_reaction: Na₂CO₃ + H₂O + CO₂ → 2NaHCO₃; 2NaHCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + CO₂ + H₂O

concepts.name: Biological Importance of Sodium and Potassium
concepts.explanation: A 70 kg man contains ~90 g Na and ~170 g K. Na⁺ is mainly in blood plasma and interstitial fluid (outside cells), involved in nerve signal transmission, water regulation, and transport of sugars/amino acids. K⁺ is mainly inside cells, activates enzymes, participates in glucose oxidation, and transmits nerve signals. The Na⁺/K⁺ pump consumes >1/3 of ATP.

concepts.name: Atomic and Ionic Radii of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metals have smaller atomic and ionic radii than corresponding alkali metals in the same period due to increased nuclear charge. Radii increase down the group from Be to Ba.

concepts.name: Ionisation Enthalpy of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metals have low ionization enthalpies due to fairly large size. First ionization enthalpy decreases down the group. They are higher than corresponding alkali metals due to smaller size. Second ionization enthalpies are smaller than those of corresponding alkali metals.

concepts.name: Hydration Enthalpy of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Hydration enthalpies decrease down the group: Be²⁺ > Mg²⁺ > Ca²⁺ > Sr²⁺ > Ba²⁺. They are larger than those of alkali metal ions. Compounds are more extensively hydrated: MgCl₂·6H₂O, CaCl₂·6H₂O exist, while NaCl and KCl do not form hydrates.

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metals are silvery white, lustrous, relatively soft but harder than alkali metals. Melting and boiling points are higher than corresponding alkali metals. They are strongly electropositive (increases down the group). Ca (brick red), Sr (crimson), Ba (apple green) give flame colours; Be and Mg do not.

concepts.name: Flame Test of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Ca, Sr, and Ba impart characteristic flame colours: Ca – brick red, Sr – crimson, Ba – apple green. Be and Mg do not give flame colours because their electrons are too strongly bound to get excited. Used in qualitative analysis and flame photometry.

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Reactivity towards Air and Water
concepts.explanation: Be and Mg are kinetically inert to oxygen and water due to oxide film formation. Powdered Be burns brilliantly to give BeO and Be₃N₂. Mg burns with dazzling brilliance to give MgO and Mg₃N₂. Ca, Sr, Ba are readily attacked by air and react with water even in cold.
concepts.formula_reaction: 2M + O₂ → 2MO; 3M + N₂ → M₃N₂; M + 2H₂O → M(OH)₂ + H₂

concepts.name: Chemical Properties – Reactivity towards Halogens and Hydrogen
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metals react with halogens to form halides MX₂. BeCl₂ is made from BeO + C + Cl₂. They combine with hydrogen (except Be) to form hydrides MH₂. BeH₂ is prepared by the reaction of BeCl₂ with LiAlH₄.
concepts.formula_reaction: M + X₂ → MX₂; M + H₂ → MH₂ (except Be); 2BeCl₂ + LiAlH₄ → 2BeH₂ + LiCl + AlCl₃

concepts.name: Reducing Nature of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metals are strong reducing agents (large negative E° values), but less powerful than corresponding alkali metals. Be has a less negative value but is a strong reducing agent due to high hydration energy of Be²⁺ and high atomization enthalpy.

concepts.name: Solutions of Alkaline Earth Metals in Liquid Ammonia
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metals dissolve in liquid ammonia to give deep blue-black solutions forming ammoniated ions: M + (x+y)NH₃ → [M(NH₃)x]²⁺ + 2[e(NH₃)y]⁻. Ammoniates [M(NH₃)₆]²⁺ can be recovered.

concepts.name: Uses of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Be: alloys (Cu-Be for high strength springs), windows of X-ray tubes. Mg: alloys with Al, Zn, Mn, Sn for aircraft construction; flash powders; milk of magnesia (antacid). Ca: extraction of metals from oxides, removal of air from vacuum tubes. Ra salts: radiotherapy.

concepts.name: Oxides and Hydroxides of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metals burn in oxygen to form MO (except BeO, which is covalent and amphoteric). Other oxides are ionic and basic. Hydroxides are sparingly soluble; solubility, thermal stability, and basicity increase down the group. Be(OH)₂ is amphoteric.

concepts.name: Halides of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Beryllium halides are covalent and soluble in organic solvents. BeCl₂ has a chain structure in solid and a chloro-bridged dimer in vapour. Hydrate formation decreases down the group: MgCl₂·8H₂O, CaCl₂·6H₂O, SrCl₂·6H₂O, BaCl₂·2H₂O. Fluorides are less soluble than chlorides due to high lattice energy.

concepts.name: Carbonates of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Alkaline earth metal carbonates (MCO₃) are insoluble in water. Solubility decreases down the group. They decompose on heating to give MO and CO₂. Thermal stability increases with increasing cationic size. BeCO₃ is unstable and can be kept only in a CO₂ atmosphere.

concepts.name: Sulphates of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: BeSO₄ and MgSO₄ are readily soluble in water; solubility decreases from CaSO₄ to BaSO₄. Greater hydration enthalpies of Be²⁺ and Mg²⁺ overcome lattice enthalpy, making them soluble.

concepts.name: Nitrates of Alkaline Earth Metals
concepts.explanation: Nitrates are made by dissolving carbonates in dilute HNO₃. Mg(NO₃)₂·6H₂O crystallises; Ba(NO₃)₂ is anhydrous. All decompose on heating to give the oxide: 2M(NO₃)₂ → 2MO + 4NO₂ + O₂.

concepts.name: Anomalous Behaviour of Beryllium
concepts.explanation: Be shows anomalous behaviour due to its small size and high ionization enthalpy. Compounds are largely covalent. Be does not exhibit coordination number >4. BeO and Be(OH)₂ are amphoteric. Be shows diagonal relationship to Al.

concepts.name: Diagonal Relationship between Beryllium and Aluminium
concepts.explanation: Be and Al show diagonal relationship due to similar ionic radii (Be²⁺ 31 pm, Al³⁺ 53.5 pm) and charge/radius ratios. Similarities: both are not readily attacked by acids (oxide film), hydroxides dissolve in excess alkali (beryllate and aluminate ions), chlorides are covalent, Lewis acids, and form complexes.

concepts.name: Calcium Oxide (Quick Lime) – Preparation and Uses
concepts.explanation: CaO is prepared by heating limestone (CaCO₃) in a rotary kiln at 1070-1270 K. It is a white amorphous solid (m.p. 2870 K). It absorbs moisture and CO₂. Slaking with water gives Ca(OH)₂. Uses: cement manufacture, alkali, purification of sugar, dye stuffs.
concepts.formula_reaction: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (heat); CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂; CaO + CO₂ → CaCO₃

concepts.name: Calcium Hydroxide (Slaked Lime)
concepts.explanation: Ca(OH)₂ is prepared by adding water to quick lime. It is a white amorphous powder, sparingly soluble in water. Aqueous solution is lime water; suspension is milk of lime. It reacts with CO₂ to give CaCO₃ (milky). Uses: mortar, white wash, glass making, tanning, bleaching powder, sugar purification.
concepts.formula_reaction: Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O; CaCO₃ + CO₂ + H₂O → Ca(HCO₃)₂

concepts.name: Calcium Carbonate – Occurrence and Uses
concepts.explanation: CaCO₃ occurs as limestone, chalk, and marble. It is a white fluffy powder, almost insoluble in water. On heating to 1200 K, it decomposes to CaO and CO₂. Reacts with dilute acids to liberate CO₂. Uses: building material, quick lime manufacture, flux in metal extraction, antacid, toothpaste, cosmetics.
concepts.formula_reaction: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (1200K); CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂

concepts.name: Calcium Sulphate (Plaster of Paris)
concepts.explanation: Plaster of Paris (CaSO₄·½H₂O) is obtained by heating gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) to 393 K. Above 393 K, anhydrous CaSO₄ (dead burnt plaster) is formed. It sets with water to form a hard solid in 5-15 minutes. Uses: building industry, immobilising fractures, dentistry, ornamental work.

concepts.name: Portland Cement – Manufacture and Composition
concepts.explanation: Cement is made by heating a mixture of limestone (CaO) and clay (SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃, MgO) in a rotary kiln to form cement clinker. The clinker is mixed with 2-3% gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O). Average composition: CaO 50-60%, SiO₂ 20-25%, Al₂O₃ 5-10%, MgO 2-3%, Fe₂O₃ 1-2%, SO₃ 1-2%. Ingredients: dicalcium silicate (26%), tricalcium silicate (51%), tricalcium aluminate (11%).

concepts.name: Setting of Cement
concepts.explanation: When mixed with water, cement sets to a hard mass due to hydration and rearrangement of molecules. Gypsum is added to slow down the setting process so that the cement gets sufficiently hardened. Uses: concrete, reinforced concrete, plastering, bridges, dams, buildings.

concepts.name: Biological Importance of Magnesium and Calcium
concepts.explanation: An adult body contains ~25 g Mg and ~1200 g Ca. Mg is a cofactor for all enzymes using ATP in phosphate transfer; it is present in chlorophyll (light absorption in plants). Ca (99% in bones and teeth) plays roles in neuromuscular function, interneuronal transmission, cell membrane integrity, and blood coagulation. Calcium concentration in plasma is ~100 mg L⁻¹, regulated by calcitonin and parathyroid hormone.
